meta.title: Mercedes E-Class W211 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class W211 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes E-Class W211 — from the 1.8‑litre M271 Kompressor four‑cylinder to the 6.2‑litre M156 AMG V8, plus the OM648 straight‑six and OM642 V6 diesels that made this generation a legend in the UK taxi and fleet trades. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the 2006 facelift that dropped the troublesome SBC brakes and brought new V6s. Backed by 276+ real UK W211 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class W211 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W211 specifically — from the pre‑facelift M112 V6 and OM648 straight‑six diesel to the facelift M272 V6 and OM642 V6 diesel.

overview.intro: The W211 is the third generation of the Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class, produced from 2001 to 2009 and offered in saloon (W211) and estate (S211) body styles. Launched as the successor to the W210, it introduced a more aerodynamic design, a longer wheelbase, and a range of new engines including the OM648 straight‑six diesel that would become a favourite of UK taxi fleets. A significant April 2006 facelift brought new powertrains (M272 V6, OM642 V6 diesel), the deletion of the troublesome SBC brake system, and updated styling. At the top of the range sat the E 55 AMG — a 476bhp supercharged V8 executive express — later replaced by the 514bhp E 63 AMG with the naturally aspirated M156 V8.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W211 (shared with S211 estate) • Predecessor: W210 • Successor: W212 • Sibling body styles in this generation: S211 (estate)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W211 E‑Class generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the OM642 and OM648 diesel variants as these ageing executive cars enter high‑mileage third‑or‑fourth‑owner territory.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre straight‑six diesel — 204 bhp, 500 Nm of torque, and the most reliable diesel ever fitted to a W211. The OM648/722.6 drivetrain combo is absolutely bulletproof — examples regularly exceed 400,000 miles on the original engine and gearbox. The OM648 is widely considered one of the best engines Mercedes ever built.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want bulletproof diesel reliability and effortless six‑cylinder torque — the one to buy.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre V6 — 224 bhp, 315 Nm of torque, and the last of Mercedes' simple 3‑valve‑per‑cylinder V6s. No balance‑shaft issues, no SBC problems (on the engine itself), just smooth, reliable V6 power. The M112 is widely considered one of the best engines Mercedes ever built. If you can find one with a clean history, the powertrain itself is unlikely to give you grief.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want pre‑facelift V6 refinement without the M272's balance‑shaft concerns.

items.quote: The M272 V6 is a significant step forward in power and refinement, but early units (2005–2007, up to engine number 30 468 993) suffer from balance‑shaft sprocket wear — a £2,000‑£3,000 repair that requires engine removal. A worn sprocket can destroy the engine if ignored. The 3.5‑litre E 350 is the worst affected.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand the M272's reputation and budget for balance‑shaft replacement — or buy a 2008‑2009 car where the issue was resolved.

items.quote: The 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 is a masterpiece, but when it fails — cylinder head bolts (up to engine code 156983 60 060658), camshaft adjusters, or valve stem seals — the repair bills are in a different league. The M156 is definitely less reliable and higher maintenance than the M113K.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand that 514 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — 134–150 bhp, 340 Nm of torque, and the most common diesel in the UK W211 parc. It's a reliable, albeit slow, lump, and the 5‑speed 722.6 gearbox will also go on forever. The cheapest W211 to run and insure.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want E‑Class prestige without the six‑cylinder running costs.

items.quote: The 2.6‑litre V6 is often overshadowed by the 3.2‑litre E 320, but it's the same durable M112 family with lower running costs. No balance‑shaft issues, no SBC problems — just honest V6 motoring. The M112 and M113 need some maintenance, being 14 or more years old, but they remain some of the most reliable Mercedes engines ever built.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement on a budget.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Value — more power, same costs
AMG Value — reliable V8, AMG costs

economicsRule.title: The W211 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200–£3,800 OM646 recon against a £2,500–£8,000 W211 sits at 28–152% of the car's value. On a sub‑£3,000 W211, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £5,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 44–76% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, and always budget for SBC brake system maintenance on pre‑facelift cars — the biggest non‑engine cost on this generation.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W211 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM646.961 (E 220 CDI) — cheap to run, reliable, common parts, 722.6 gearbox is bulletproof.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W211 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M156.983 (E 63 AMG) — V8 running costs, AMG parts prices, head bolt and camshaft adjuster risks.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W211 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM648.961 (E 320 CDI) — bulletproof straight‑six diesel, 400,000+ mile proven, and the most desirable diesel engine in the W211 range.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Wear (Facelift V6)
problems.recommendation: On any M272 W211, check the engine number against the affected range — early units (2005–2007) are at risk. £2,000–£3,000 now is far cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 replacement that follows a failure. Post‑2008 cars are significantly improved.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (Facelift Diesel)
problems.recommendation: On any OM642 W211 over 80,000 miles, inspect for oil leaks around the V of the engine — £800–£1,400 now is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows if oil loss causes engine damage.

problems.title: SBC Brake System (Pre‑Facelift, 2002–2006)
problems.recommendation: On any pre‑facelift W211, check SBC service history — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £1,500–£2,500 replacement that follows a complete failure. Facelift cars (2006–2009) dropped SBC entirely.

problems.title: W211 Rust & Subframe Corrosion
problems.recommendation: Inspect the rear wheel arches, sill undersides and subframe mounting points carefully before purchase — £400–£800 now is far cheaper than the £1,200–£2,500 repair that follows if corrosion spreads. This is the biggest non‑engine, non‑SBC issue on the W211.

replacementCosts.h2: W211 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M271, M112, M272, M113, M156, OM646, OM647, OM648 or OM642 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines, adjusted for the W211's executive‑car labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W211 saloon and S211 estate. The saloon and estate share all engines, with only minor labour differences for some repairs. The estate's additional weight and rear suspension setup can affect fuel economy and running costs.
